content: @Xeanor Regarding SLYA spam, one of the SA guys was nice enough to review some key functions with me.  I hope to have an update before the end of the year. I'd be really curious to see a "before" and "after" on whatever measurables are available.  One item of particular interest is the concept of RPC automatic retries vs client-side retries. I think this is the big one that's typically deemed "spam", but I suspect this method might actually be more efficient from a resource consumption perspective.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: Supply Chain Attack Detected in solana/web3.js Library

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content:
Source: socket.dev/blog/supply-chain-attack-solana-web3-js-library

"This is not an issue with the Solana protocol itself, but with a specific JavaScript client library and only appears to affect projects that directly handle private keys and that updated within the window of 3:20pm UTC and 8:25pm UTC on Tuesday, December 2, 2024."   (//socket.dev/blog/malicious-npm-package-targets-solana-developers-and-hijacks-funds)

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: Ustur has objectively always been the least balanced faction. If you use inconsequential factors to measure you can frame any faction as the best or worst. The measure is simple. You look at the most central aspects that are required for every other action. 4R, particularly Ammo and fuel. food requirements for fleets are minimal. Tools and the majority of food is less important since upkeep is shared by many users. it doesn't matter how good your layout is, if you don't have ammo of fuel you can't use it. Ustur is the only faction that has not had access to ammo on half their map.

author: [AEP] Bodhitree
content: they also don't have iron on the same side which isn't as important as copper, but its far more important than most of the commonly discussed discrepancies

author: 《VΛ》 KATIREcon Vantarah
content:
I'm posting here my twitt with a brewing thoughts on the coming RFR...

Part I.
I'd like to propose an improvement to the RFR (Request for Resources) dynamic in Star Atlas. Currently, this mechanic works in one direction: you craft the RFR, and you can either hold it or convert it into $ATLAS tokens right away.

WHAT IF we make the RFR BACKWARDS-CONVERTABLE.

This means an RFR could be deconstructed back into its original crafting materials: fuel, food, ammo, toolkits, and SDUs. let's call this new version RFRbc (Backwards-Convertible RFR) in the context of that proposal.

Here’s why this could matter:,It would help stabilize the in-game market prices for R4 (fuel, food, ammo, toolkit) and SDUs (space-discovered units) in $ATLAS terms.,It introduces new, dynamic market opportunities—but also some strategic trade-offs.,
How it could work,That mechanics could to a certain degree stabilize the prices of the R4 and SDUs in $atlas terms. This has pros and cons of course...

And that is where all that becomes even more interesting - to prevent the PEG being too tight we could add some variables/conditions to the RFRbc:

Forward Expiry Date: RFRbc would have a deadline for conversion into $ATLAS.,Backwards Expiry Date: A separate deadline for converting RFRbc back into R4 and SDUs.,Conversion Delay: Newly created RFRbc would require a waiting period before it can be converted in either direction.,

author: 《VΛ》 KATIREcon Vantarah
content:
Part II.
Adding Depth
These conditions could offer various timeframes, for example: 2 weeks, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months, 12 months, or even up to 3 years. The shorter the delay or the longer the expiry, the more expensive the crafting fee. This creates an ARBITRAGE OPPORTUNITY for players but also ensures balance.

And that leads us to the next point:
RFRbc Crafting Quotes: to manage supply the number of RFRbc that can be crafted wouldn’t be completely infinite. Instead, there would be thresholds where crafting becomes progressively more expensive or cheaper, depending on market demand.,Actually, this point #4 is the one I am not sure about - SA looks for the smaller players - would that dry up the interest of smaller participants of the dynamics...

Why It Matters,These mechanics could:

Enhance DAO treasury profits through variable crafting fees.,Introduce strategic decision-making to the market.,Prevent unlimited exploitation of conversion opportunities, ensuring stability.,Level-up the Star Atlas marketplace into something akin to a FIRST VIRTUAL EXCHANGE WITH OPTIONS-like mechanics.,
This opens the door to new participants: traders and investors attracted by the market dynamics. Just like the Faction Fleet launch brought in waves of new players, cRFR could draw an even broader audience at a lower entry cost but with deeper market potential.

This proposal could be submitted to PolisDAO for approval.
Let's make RFR GREAT AGAIN.

Let me know what you think!

author: Odicerous
content:
Five years is a looong time.  But its not forever.  Thats one more lever for a player to consider. Short term lock, mid term lock, or long term.
Once you perma lock, that part of the game is over.  Done.
And I worry it will create more disparity.  Small players may think they can use it to lever up, but I don't think so.  The largest Polis holders will be forced to perma-lock a big bag, but they will still have plenty to trade around.

author: Lysimach | Make Polis Great [STAR]
content: To me, that's kind of like a burn mechanic.  Good for meme tokens, but I think of Polis differently.  Yeah, I'd love to see the value continue to rise, but not artificially.  IMO, Polis' long term value should be 100% utility driven.  Part of that utility is its ability to be locked/unlocked/traded.

reply.preview: To me, that's kind of like a burn mechanic. Good for meme tokens, but I think of Polis differently. Yeah, I'd love to see the value continue to rise, but not artificially. IMO, Polis' long term value should be 100% utility driven. Part of that utility is its ability to be locked/unlocked/traded.

author: Odicerous
content:
One final concern.  If new players perma-lock, then want out later, the only option will be to sell/transfer their wallets.  I think this incentivizes a detrimental practice. Yes, then can do that now, but knowing they can unlock in a couple of years is more likely to keep them engaged, coming back, and participating at some level.  With perma-lock, there will be wholesale wallets sales.  I think that may undermine the economy in ways we can't yet predict.
I just really like current Polis system as designed.  Pretty brilliant really.And we have yet to scratch the surface on what Polis ownership can actually be.
I'd like to see how it works through a few seasons before there are any alterations.

author: [COEX] BulutONI
content:
This would also contribute to the system functioning more effectively.
For example, if I want to buy a ship but don’t have enough funds, I could urgently withdraw my staked tokens and make the purchase. However, having to wait 21 days to access my tokens will deprive me of many opportunities. I stake my tokens mainly to reduce my fees and escape the current situation.

Due to these fees, there’s an overwhelming amount of transactions happening outside. Everyone is trying to trade without paying fees. I believe this system could at least partially address this issue.

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content:
The whole point here is that the marketplace fee would be meaningless if anyone could stake ATLAS for an hour, get the max fee reduction and then unstake again.

There is no benefit for the players to have their ATLAS locked for an additional 21 days, that is true. It is simply the “cost” of doing business and what you agree to if you want to enjoy the fee reduction and POLIS payout.

author: XeanorONI
content: We're working on it. Thought we had fixed this issue way before release already. The cause is that for setting up a sell order, your cargo gets converted into certificates. The actual trade is in certificates (SDU @ ONI-5 if we could call it that) and when you cancel an order, not only is the certificate removed from the market but they also need to be converted back to actual cargo. That conversion to cargo can fail. The inventory is set up to show both the balance of the certificates in case you end up with them as well as the actual cargo, but somehow that's not working for everyone. Looking into it.

The inventory is set up to show both the balance of the certificates in case you end up with them as well as the actual cargo, but somehow that's not working for everyone. Looking into it.

reply.preview: We're working on it. Thought we had fixed this issue way before release already. The cause is that for setting up a sell order, your cargo gets converted into certificates. The actual trade is in certificates (SDU @ ONI-5 if we could call it that) and when you cancel an order, not only is the certificate removed from the market but they also need to be converted back to actual cargo. That conversion to cargo can fail. The inventory is set up to show both the balance of the certificates in case you end up with them as well as the actual cargo, but somehow that's not working for everyone. Looking into it.

reply.preview: Does this same thing happen on a transaction that makes it part way then fails?

author: XeanorONI
content: But the "In my inventory" is coded so that it shows the sum of your certificates + cargo

author: XeanorONI
content: and if you'd create a new order, it would use the certificates first to list those, and then convert additional cargo if necessary

author: [Peace DAC] TGI LizardMUD
content: Yes, team is aware that people can do that. There was some discussion of using fees based on the market value of items in these situations.   But unknown if they have the capacity to build that in right now. Or if they even want to.  I would just encourage everyone to use the system as is. If you want less fees, use the atlas locker.    Going OTC to avoid the local market system will only hurt yourself and the game.  It's less money to the DAO and ATMTA.  It fucks with the statistics of who and how much the local market is being used. E.g. less cool stats.   And practically, it makes the markets feel dead.  This is a snowball effect. The more people in the markets the more people will come.  Tl;Dr bypassing local markets is only shooting yourself in the foot.

author: [SAG] Swift42 [CODE]
content:
In case someone had the problem that an offer wasn't created in the local market:
The RSS are now back and are correctly displayed in the Sage UI, but EveEye and SLYA can't see them.
That's because the RSS are still in a special token account (exchanged for "SPL tokens").
You can fix it by manually loading some of the RSS onto a freighter.
Then all RSS are correctly moved back to the correct token account.
I had 5M Lumanite missing and I moved 600k to a freighter, this moved the full 5M (previously missing) Lumanite  back (4.4 to the original token account, 600K to the freighter). Now EveEye and SLYA have again full access to the RSS.

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content:
@Lorddo 

 

The Starbase upgrade bonuses are intact, it's just that the base amount went down from 6 to 4.5 million.
I get that this is sudden, but the same would have happened over time if the player base would double (for example - assuming mostly minnows and not whales).

Of course, the whole issue with LP was that there was no incentive to bring in new players on the side of the current players. After all, this would just diminish their own earnings. That is why a change like this (FIC) was necessary, to pull us out of that deadlock.

The DAO is not yet in charge of game development, so they would have not been able to play a role. Which may a good thing, because players would again be incentivized to vote against this change, purely out of greed  

Not sure how you would have wanted to solve this otherwise, but love to hear your thoughts on this mate!

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: You bothered with upgrading because that is what got you the ATLAS beforehand. I don't really think anyone did it because of the bonus emissions it would unlock, given how low they were   That was.. just a bonus..  So, I do not understand this argument. If you're arguing that your whole operations have been built around upgrading Starbases, then that would make a better argument, but an arguably still a foolish one. We always knew more gameplay (and bog changes) would be coming, and strategies would have to be revised. And everyone should expect this to happen multiple times over the course of the game's development. And hopefully still every now and then after the launch of Season 8.  That FICs are an easier path to ATLAS than LP is something the team can balance. But, of course, this is to be expected at this point in time as well as LP contributions are still on their way down (increasing the contributor's ATLAS reward) now that FICs have been around for less than 24 hours.

author: [HEIM] CapThor
content: When I reported it, low fee was ~100k mL/CU. You should get a tx through with a low fee (it takes some time more). Most Sage txs are around 50k CU. 100k mL * 50k mL / 1M = 5000 lamports So 5k lamports should've been enough. But even with 20k nothing worked. In the meantime the fees increased, low is now ~250k mL/CU, so theoretically 12k lamports should work.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
There might be a case for this. However, worth emphasizing that we fully anticipate local markets to be disconnected from the GM. There is value in having a resources in deep space, and very reasonable to have a premium. Now, that's not to say it should be an order of magnitude higher, and I agree we don't want people getting tricked because of decimal precision.

With respect to your question about IC Contracts "function," it can be explained in Lore. Realistically, we're leveraging that as an emission mechanism. But if you want to be immersed, just imagine that these SBs require supply lines to properly participate in this galactic war. If they aren't supplied with the materials required, they're going to get captured. So that's why players are being rewarded by the factions for their wartime support.

author: Buzzec
content: Ships don't work quite the same as resources, because they aren't in your inventory. At least if you want them to be enlisted in fleets. If you've ever crafted a ship, you know you had to withdraw that asset, then redeposit via Port of Entry. There are some complexities that would need to be sorted, and wallet security considered before we could make this happen. @Buzzec could likely explain it better than I did.

author: [S&B] Sig
content: There might be a case for this. However, worth emphasizing that we fully anticipate local markets to be disconnected from the GM. There is value in having a resources in deep space, and very reasonable to have a premium. Now, that's not to say it should be an order of magnitude higher, and I agree we don't want people getting tricked because of decimal precision.  With respect to your question about IC Contracts "function," it can be explained in Lore. Realistically, we're leveraging that as an emission mechanism. But if you want to be immersed, just imagine that these SBs require supply lines to properly participate in this galactic war. If they aren't supplied with the materials required, they're going to get captured. So that's why players are being rewarded by the factions for their wartime support.

Hello all,

If you're experiencing an issue with resources or interacting with certain ones on Local Markets. Do the following steps to fix the issue: 

1 - have at least 1 fuel from another source.(aka ship that has 1 fuel for example)
2 - put that 1 fuel into affected starbase inventory
3 - load 2 fuel into your fuel tank
4 - Hard refresh "SLY" 
5-  Print

author: ROME | wikiwikiwik
content: ATMTA doesn't collect any fees on crafting. But out of curiosity, component prices "tanked" using which reference rate? Local markets, or GM? Going to give rise to an interesting problem for the team to solve, given price disparity across all LMs. And I was surprised to find out today, from econ team, that people are pricing resources at a discount on local markets. I was fully anticipating premiums. Now, I can see how a discount could be justified, given the seller doesn't have to pull it out through a CSS, but likewise, now a buyer doesn't need to transport it to their destination.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: It all comes down to your individual freight costs, right? The idea is that I can take advantage of LM by "paying" someone else to freight, with the assumption that they can do so more efficiently than I can.  I think the problem right now is that a large number of people already have the most efficient freight to support their operations.  The people that can freight are already doing so. The people that need freight are probably already committed to other operations (such as scanning).

author: [AEP] Mendizo
content: I had this, too. Each action required 2 signings. And now txs are (seemingly) instantly confirmed (according to the Sage toast message), but in reality some txs are often not executed (e.g. load/unload). It looks like Sage thinks that there is an instant confirmation, while in reality the tx may get confirmed in 5, 10 or 20 seconds - or never. So you may think "wow, that is superfast", while in reality it is just a wrongly displayed message. Currently you don't even know if your tx got through. Also I don't see RSS/ships in my wallet. Strange things happen here.

author: [AEP] Siphaawal
content: Problem is: Currently Sage thinks there is a instant tx confirmation, but this is untrue. So if you are doing a complex action (which e.g. consists of 3 tx), all 3 txs are sent in the same moment (instead in a subsequent order). When a validator then picks up tx 2 before tx 1, everything fails. A Solana congestion makes to problem even worse. I have to resend a crafting action up to 30 times - but eventually it works.

reply.preview: Problem is: Currently Sage thinks there is a instant tx confirmation, but this is untrue. So if you are doing a complex action (which e.g. consists of 3 tx), all 3 txs are sent in the same moment (instead in a subsequent order). When a validator then picks up tx 2 before tx 1, everything fails. A Solana congestion makes to problem even worse. I have to resend a crafting action up to 30 times - but eventually it works.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
[AEP] Siphaawal
content: @Santi good evening.  this is no rush but wanted to send this off while it was fresh in my mind.  I'm wondering if it would be a good idea to pause changing of the stations that supply FICs.   The current success rate of selling FICs is dismally low.  I know its because of Solana congestion, e.g. 2 tx and the second tx is failing. Not your fault.  And I know a few people have stopped trying to sell them until a fix is implemented.  It's just going to be a giant loss if people shipped their FICs to a station, coudn't sell them, then the station changes, and they have to ship them elsewhere again.  tl;dr Solana is FICing everyone.  Pls pause station changes.  Thank you!  p.s. for anyone that read this far - FIC sell success rate is higher when the U.S. is asleep and/or when priority fees are super low.  1) Redeem your certificates, 2) hit refresh at the top of the local market, 3) sell.   4) repeat until it goes through.

reply.preview: @Santi good evening. this is no rush but wanted to send this off while it was fresh in my mind. I'm wondering if it would be a good idea to pause changing of the stations that supply FICs. The current success rate of selling FICs is dismally low. I know its because of Solana congestion, e.g. 2 tx and the second tx is failing. Not your fault. And I know a few people have stopped trying to sell them until a fix is implemented. It's just going to be a giant loss if people shipped their FICs to a station, coudn't sell them, then the station changes, and they have to ship them elsewhere again. tl;dr Solana is FICing everyone. Pls pause station changes. Thank you! p.s. for anyone that read this far - FIC sell success rate is higher when the U.S. is asleep and/or when priority fees are super low. 1) Redeem your certificates, 2) hit refresh at the top of the local market, 3) sell. 4) repeat until it goes through.

author: ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics [STAR]
content: Is there anything in the solana development roadmap which will solves or elevate these issue in your opinion? Asking as you seem knowledgeable about the sol ecosystem.

reply.preview: Is there anything in the solana development roadmap which will solves or elevate these issue in your opinion? Asking as you seem knowledgeable about the sol ecosystem.

author: [AEP] Siphaawal
content: I mean I was the #1 Solana Validator for a few days.  Now I'm mostly top 10.  I am pretty knowledgeable.   Yes, there is tons and tons of bits on the road map.  Solana is faster then it's ever been.  But here's the problem.   For the very reasons that Star Atlas chose solana. Everyone else is too.  So there is competition.  And more usage means higher priority fees.  That being said Solana is still infinitely cheaper than other comparable chains.  So Solana needs to outpace demand.  And they will.    So many great minds working on this problem.

author: [SAG] Swift42 [CODE]
content:
I changed the default values of SLYA in one of the latest version:
FeeMin: 1
TimeMin: 10
FeeMax: 10000
TimeMax: 70
Max fee change/tx: 80
That's a good starting point. If you want to decrease the calculated fee, easiest way to do it is to decrease FeeMax (e.g. I use 8000 in my second instance). But keep in mind that the auto fee needs some room to work, so setting FeeMax to e.g. 500 or 1000 would be pointless.
Also if you have crafting jobs: Crafting txs are super heavy and need a lot of priority fee to have the same speed as the other txs. But if you are OK with a longer wait time here, you can save a lot of Sol (because the priority fee is applied to each tx instruction and the crafting txs have up to 5 instructions). The default value of "Fee multiplier for crafting transactions" is 200%, but you can set it to e.g. 100% or 125%. If you do this and if you use "Auto-Fee", don't include crafting transactions in the fee calculation then, because the long running crafting txs would increase the auto fee a lot.

author: [AEP] Lerinor [STAR]
content:
I'm doing 20 for min, and 75 for max. When it spikes more (SOL congestion), I set it to 30/90. This gives it more room to lower fees, and raises it less often.
I have 27 fleets running (1 heavy freighter on warp, 7 subwarpers, 4 scanners, rest is mining), and am spending about 0.06 - 0.08 SOL/day, including heavy congested days on that average.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: Just look at the VWAP differences with a set ship class and then compare the parts tiers.  Since I already picked up a bunch I will provide you guys with an example: JetJet vs. X5.  JetJet is approximately $160 higher in VWAP due to T3 parts so the JetJet parts have an approximate VWAP of $160.  Now since Jetjet prices are roughly 95% below VWAP currently for ships, a good price for JetJet parts would be anything below $8 ($160 * 5%), but I would say anything below $5 is currently worth the snipe for now.  You can use that same principle for other ships as well.  edit: for more accurate pricing due to JetJet example being 95% below VWAP

author: [SAG] Swift42 [CODE]
content:
In case someone has the same problem:
I wondered why Sage executes txs in less than 5 seconds, while SLYA needs 30 to 40 seconds.
Then I realized that Sage is using crazy fees right now. For me it plays no role if I enable or disable priority fees in Sage.
E.g. I warped with a fleet and paid 0.03 USD for a single warp.
So currently it costs an arm and a leg to operate my fleets in Sage.
